Love one another intensely from the heart.—.

Jehovah sets the example for us. His love is so intense that if we are loyal to him, nothing can break that bond. The Greek word rendered “intensely” conveys the idea of stretching outward—even straining. At times, we may need to “stretch” and “strain” in order to have tender affection for a fellow believer. When others upset us, we need to continue “putting up with one another in love, earnestly endeavoring to maintain the oneness of the spirit in the uniting bond of peace.” We will look beyond our brothers’ faults and try our best to view our brothers as Jehovah does. It is not always easy to show tender affection for our brothers and sisters, especially when we are aware of their faults. Apparently, this was a challenge for some first-century Christians, as was the case with Euodia and Syntyche. The apostle Paul urged them “to be of the same mind in the Lord.”—. w21.0122-23 ¶10-11

reference: As the “Lamb” opens the third seal, “a black horse” charges forth. “And the one seated upon it had a pair of scales in his hand.” Ah, here is a companion rider to the horseman of total warfare! This is the horseman that brings famine. During both the world wars, famine conditions plagued many countries. Food rationing, as represented by the “pair of scales,” became the norm for the citizens of the warring nations. And in the wake of World War I came the greatest famine of all history. The Nationof June 7, 1919, reported that 32,000,000 people in India were “on the verge of starvation.”World’s Workof March 1921 stated that in northern China alone 15,000 people were dying every day from hunger. The New YorkTimespublicationCurrent History Magazineof October 1921 quoted a British report that in Russia “no fewer than 35,000,000 people are stalked by the grim spectre of famine and pestilence.” Similar famine conditions prevailed following World War II, whenLookmagazine of June 11, 1946, reported: “A fourth of the world is starving today.”

reference:
E tonu, e mafai o faigatā a te fakamalosiga ke mōmea aka te iloa ‵lei ne koe a koe eiloa, kae konei a nīsi manatu fesoasoani kolā e mafai o fesoasoani mai:

1. Fakatoka a Fakamoemoega: Fakatoka a fakamoemoega aoga kolā e mafai o fakataunu ne koe. E pelā me se fakaakoakoga, kāfai e manako koe ke mōmea aka tou iloa ‵lei e uiga ki ou lagonaga, fakatoka se fakamoemoega ke iloa kae taku mai se lagonaga e tasi i aso takitasi.

2. Ke Fakamau Tou Gasolo ki Mua: Ke fakamau tou gasolo ki mua i se tusi, se polokalame i te tivaisi, io me fakaasi atu ki se tino telā e talitonu koe ki ei. A te kilo atu ki tou gasolo ki mua e mafai o fakamālosi kae fakatumau ne ia tou loto finafinau mō se taimi leva.

3. Fakamau a Fakamasauaga: Fakamau a fakamasauaga i tou koga galue io me i tau telefoni e pelā me se fakailoga ke fakamasaua atu ki a koe ke fai a mea kolā e manako koe o iloa ‵lei ei a koe eiloa.

4. Fakamanuia a Tamā Manumalōga: Fakamanuia a tamā manumalōga, ke fakamautinoa aka a tou manuia i te faiga tenā, telā e mafai o fesoasoani atu ke fakatumau tou loto finafinau mō se taimi leva.

5. ‵Sala se Soa: A te mauaga o se soa ke ‵lago atu ki a koe e mafai o fesoasoani atu ke tumau koe i te faiga o te mea tenā, ke mōmea aka tou galue mālosi ki ei, kae fai foki pelā me se mea fakafiafia.

6. Fai Pelā me se Mea Masani: A te fakafetaui aka o te mea telā e manako koe o iloa ‵lei ei a koe eiloa ki au mea māsani i aso takitasi e mafai o fesoasoani atu ke fai pelā me se faifaiga masani kae tumau i ei.

7. Filifili a Mea Kolā e Fiafia Koe ki ei: A te filifili aka o mea kolā e fiafia koe ki ei io me fai pelā me se mea fakafiafia e mafai o fesoasoani atu ke fakatumau tou loto finafinau kae galue mālosi ki ei mō se taimi leva.

Masaua, a te atiakaga o te iloa ‵lei ne koe a koe eiloa se faigāmea, kae e tāua ke fakatoka se palani telā e aogā ki a koe!
